   622→        in
   623→        if cr1 = result_nothing && cr2 = result_nothing then skip := true);
   624→      if
   625→        (not !skip)
   626→        && is_d2
   627→        && obj_type_ob = obj_powerup
   628→        && flags land fq_ignore_powerups <> 0
   629→      then skip := true;
   630→      if (not !skip) && thisobjnum > -1
   631→      then (
   632→        if thisobj_type = obj_robot && obj_type_ob = obj_robot
   633→        then
   634→          if is_d2
   635→          then skip := true
   636→          else if not (attack_type_ob <> 0 && thisobj_attack <> 0)
   637→          then skip := true);
   638→      if not !skip
   639→      then (
   640→        let fudged_rad = ref rad in
   641→        if thisobjnum > -1
   642→        then (
   643→          if thisobj_type = obj_robot && thisobj_attack <> 0
   644→          then fudged_rad := rad * 3 / 4;
   645→          if
   646→            thisobj_type = obj_player
   647→            && (obj_type_ob = obj_player
   648→                || (game_mode_coop
   649→                    && obj_type_ob = obj_weapon
   650→                    && parent_type_ob = obj_player))
   651→          then fudged_rad := rad / 2);
   652→        let d, hit_point =
   653→          check_vector_to_object
   654→            ~p0
   655→            ~p1
   656→            ~rad:!fudged_rad
   657→            ~obj_pos:obj_pos_ob
   658→            ~obj_size:obj_size_ob
   659→            ~obj_type:obj_type_ob
   660→            ~attack_type:attack_type_ob
   661→            ~otherobj_type:thisobj_type
   662→            ~game_mode_coop
   663→            ~otherobj_parent_type:
   664→              (match thisobj_data with Some d -> d.(8) | None -> 0)
   665→        in
   666→        if d <> 0 && d < !closest_d
   667→        then (
   668→          st.fvi_hit_object <- ob;
   669→          closest_d := d;
   670→          closest_hit_point := hit_point;
   671→          hit_type := hit_object));
   672→      objnum := next_in_seg
   673→    done);
   674→  (* HACK: if this object type doesn't collide with walls, set rad=0 *)
   675→  let rad =

   678→      let cr =

   681→      if cr = result_nothing then 0 else rad)

   683→  in
   684→  (* Get start/end masks *)
   685→  let startmask =

   687→      Ox_gameseg.get_seg_masks
   688→        ~checkp:p0 ~rad ~seg_verts ~side_types ~normals ~seg_vert_positions
   689→    in
   690→    fm
   691→  in
   692→  let endmask, _sidemask, centermask =
   693→    Ox_gameseg.get_seg_masks
   694→      ~checkp:p1 ~rad ~seg_verts ~side_types ~normals ~seg_vert_positions
   695→  in
   696→  if centermask = 0 then hit_none_seg := startseg;

   699→    let bit = ref 1 in
   700→    let side = ref 0 in
   701→    while !side < 6 && endmask >= !bit && not !quit do
   702→      let sn = !side in
   703→      let num_faces_raw =
   704→        let st = side_types.(sn) in
   705→        if st = 1 then 1 else if st = 2 || st = 3 then 2 else 1
   706→      in
   707→      let num_faces = if num_faces_raw = 0 then 1 else num_faces_raw in
   708→      for face = 0 to 1 do
   709→        if (not !quit) && endmask land !bit <> 0
   710→        then
   711→          if children.(sn) <> entry_seg

   713→            let nv = if num_faces = 1 then 4 else 3 in
   714→            let _nf, vertex_list =
   715→              Ox_gameseg.create_abs_vertex_lists

   717→            in
   718→            let norm = normals.((sn * 2) + face) in
   719→            let face_hit_type, hit_point =
   720→              if startmask land !bit <> 0
   721→              then
   722→                special_check_line_to_face
   723→                  ~p0 ~p1 ~norm ~rad ~facenum:face ~nv ~num_faces
   724→                  ~side_type:side_types.(sn) ~sidenum:sn

   728→                  ~p0 ~p1 ~norm ~rad ~facenum:face ~nv ~num_faces
   729→                  ~vertex_list ~seg_verts ~seg_vert_positions
   730→            in
   731→            if face_hit_type <> 0
   732→            then (
   733→              let wid_flag =
   734→                let base_wid = wid.(sn) in
   735→                if thisobjnum = player_objnum && physics_cheat
   736→                then (
   737→                  let w = base_wid in
   738→                  if children.(sn) >= 0 then w lor wid_fly_flag else w)
   739→                else base_wid
   740→              in
   741→              let is_flyable = wid_flag land wid_fly_flag <> 0 in
   742→              let is_trans =

   744→                then
   745→                  wid_flag land wid_render_flag <> 0
   746→                  && wid_flag land wid_rendpast_flag <> 0
   747→                else wid_flag = wid_transparent_wall_d1

   749→              let passable =
   750→                is_flyable
   751→                || (is_trans && flags land fq_transwall <> 0)
   752→                || (is_trans
   753→                    && flags land fq_transpoint <> 0
   754→                    &&
   755→                    let hx, hy, hz = hit_point in

   757→              in

   762→                for i = 0 to st.n_segs_visited - 1 do

   765→                if not !already_visited

   767→                  st.segs_visited.(st.n_segs_visited) <- newsegnum;
   768→                  st.n_segs_visited <- st.n_segs_visited + 1;

   771→                  else (
   772→                    let save_wall_norm = st.wall_norm in
   773→                    let save_hit_objnum = st.fvi_hit_object in
   774→                    let ( sub_hit_type
   775→                        , sub_hit_point
   776→                        , sub_hit_seg
   777→                        , temp_seglist
   778→                        , temp_n_segs )
   779→                      =
   780→                      fvi_sub_v2
   781→                        ~collision_table ~n_objects ~is_d2 ~game_time
   782→                        ~ignore_obj_list ~game_mode_coop ~player_objnum ~physics_cheat
   783→                        st
   784→                        ~p0 ~startseg:newsegnum ~p1 ~rad ~thisobjnum ~flags
   785→                        ~entry_seg:startseg
   786→                    in
   787→                    if sub_hit_type <> hit_none
   788→                    then (
   789→                      let d = vm_vec_dist ~a:sub_hit_point ~b:p0 in
   790→                      if d < !closest_d

   792→                        closest_d := d;
   793→                        closest_hit_point := sub_hit_point;
   794→                        hit_type := sub_hit_type;
   795→                        if sub_hit_seg <> -1 then hit_seg := sub_hit_seg;
   796→                        if flags land fq_get_seglist <> 0
   797→                        then (
   798→                          let ii = ref 0 in
   799→                          while !ii < temp_n_segs && !n_segs < max_fvi_segs - 1 do
   800→                            seglist.(!n_segs) <- temp_seglist.(!ii);
   801→                            n_segs := !n_segs + 1;
   802→                            ii := !ii + 1
   803→                          done))
   804→                      else (
   805→                        st.wall_norm <- save_wall_norm;
   806→                        st.fvi_hit_object <- save_hit_objnum))
   807→                    else (
   808→                      st.wall_norm <- save_wall_norm;
   809→                      if sub_hit_seg <> -1 then hit_none_seg := sub_hit_seg;
   810→                      if flags land fq_get_seglist <> 0
   811→                      then
   812→                        for ii = 0 to min (temp_n_segs - 1) (max_fvi_segs - 2) do
   813→                          hit_none_seglist.(ii) <- temp_seglist.(ii)
   814→                        done;
   815→                      hit_none_n_segs := temp_n_segs))))
   816→              else (
   817→                let d = vm_vec_dist ~a:hit_point ~b:p0 in
   818→                if d < !closest_d

   820→                  closest_d := d;
   821→                  closest_hit_point := hit_point;
   822→                  hit_type := hit_wall;
   823→                  st.wall_norm <- normals.((sn * 2) + face);

   828→                  if cm = 0 then hit_seg := startseg else st.fvi_hit_seg2 <- startseg;

   832→        bit := !bit lsl 1
   833→      done;
   834→      incr side
   835→    done);
   836→  if !hit_type = hit_none
   837→  then (
   838→    let intp = p1 in
   839→    let ints = !hit_none_seg in
   840→    if !hit_none_seg <> -1
   841→    then (
   842→      if flags land fq_get_seglist <> 0
   843→      then
   844→        for i = 0 to !hit_none_n_segs - 1 do
   845→          if !n_segs < max_fvi_segs - 1

   847→            seglist.(!n_segs) <- hit_none_seglist.(i);
   848→            n_segs := !n_segs + 1)
   849→        done)
   850→    else if cur_nest_level <> 0
   851→    then n_segs := 0;
   852→    !hit_type, intp, ints, seglist, !n_segs)

   854→    let intp = !closest_hit_point in
   855→    let ints =

   860→    !hit_type, intp, ints, seglist, !n_segs)
   861→;;

   863→(* Unpack segment data for centermask check (returns just the fields needed) *)
   864→let unpack_seg_for_masks seg_data =
   865→  let seg_verts = Array.init 8 ~f:(fun i -> seg_data.(12 + i)) in
   866→  let side_types = Array.init 6 ~f:(fun i -> seg_data.(6 + i)) in
   867→  let normals =
   868→    Array.init 12 ~f:(fun i ->
   869→      let off = 20 + (i * 3) in
   870→      seg_data.(off), seg_data.(off + 1), seg_data.(off + 2))
   871→  in
   872→  let seg_vert_positions =
   873→    Array.init 8 ~f:(fun i ->
   874→      let off = 56 + (i * 3) in
   875→      seg_data.(off), seg_data.(off + 1), seg_data.(off + 2))
   876→  in
   877→  seg_verts, side_types, normals, seg_vert_positions
   878→;;

   880→(* find_point_seg_fvi_v2: uses Fetch_segment_data effects *)

   892→    let seg_vert_positions =
   893→      Array.init 8 ~f:(fun i ->
   894→        let off = 56 + (i * 3) in
   895→        seg_data.(off), seg_data.(off + 1), seg_data.(off + 2))
   896→    in
   897→    children, side_types, seg_verts, normals, seg_vert_positions
   898→  in
   899→  let rec trace s iterations =

   927→(* find_vector_intersection_v2: top-level FVI using on-demand effects.
   928→   Takes just the header array (18+N ints), fetches data via effects. *)
   929→let find_vector_intersection_v2 (arr : int array) =
   930→  let p0 = arr.(h_p0x), arr.(h_p0y), arr.(h_p0z) in
   931→  let startseg = arr.(h_startseg) in
   932→  let p1 = arr.(h_p1x), arr.(h_p1y), arr.(h_p1z) in
   933→  let rad = arr.(h_rad) in
   934→  let thisobjnum = arr.(h_thisobjnum) in

   936→  let n_segments = arr.(h_n_segments) in

   938→  let player_objnum = arr.(h_player_objnum) in
   939→  let physics_cheat = arr.(h_physics_cheat) <> 0 in
   940→  let game_mode_coop = arr.(h_game_mode_coop) <> 0 in
   941→  let game_time = arr.(h_game_time) in
   942→  let is_d2 = arr.(h_is_d2) <> 0 in
   943→  let ignore_count = arr.(h_ignore_obj_count) in
   944→  let ignore_obj_list = Array.init ignore_count ~f:(fun i -> arr.(h_ignore_obj_list + i)) in
   945→  let collision_table = Effect.perform Fetch_collision_table in
   946→  (* Validate startseg *)
   947→  let start_seg_data = Effect.perform (Ox_gameseg.Fetch_segment_data startseg) in
   948→  let seg_verts, side_types, normals, seg_vert_positions =
   949→    unpack_seg_for_masks start_seg_data
   950→  in
   951→  let _, _, centermask =
   952→    Ox_gameseg.get_seg_masks
   953→      ~checkp:p0 ~rad:0 ~seg_verts ~side_types ~normals ~seg_vert_positions
   954→  in
   955→  if centermask <> 0
   956→  then (
   957→    let p0x, p0y, p0z = p0 in
   958→    [| hit_bad_p0; p0x; p0y; p0z; startseg; 0; -1; 0; 0; 0; 0; 0 |])
   959→  else (
   960→    let st = make_fvi_state () in
   961→    st.segs_visited.(0) <- startseg;
   962→    st.n_segs_visited <- 1;
   963→    st.fvi_hit_seg2 <- -1;
   964→    let hit_type, hit_pnt, hit_seg2, seglist, n_segs =
   965→      fvi_sub_v2
   966→        ~collision_table ~n_objects ~is_d2 ~game_time ~ignore_obj_list
   967→        ~game_mode_coop ~player_objnum ~physics_cheat
   968→        st ~p0 ~startseg ~p1 ~rad ~thisobjnum ~flags ~entry_seg:(-2)
   969→    in
   970→    let hit_pnt_x, hit_pnt_y, hit_pnt_z = hit_pnt in

   972→    let hit_seg = ref (-1) in
   973→    if hit_seg2 <> -1
   974→    then (
   975→      let sd2 = Effect.perform (Ox_gameseg.Fetch_segment_data hit_seg2) in
   976→      let sv2, st2, n2, svp2 = unpack_seg_for_masks sd2 in
   977→      let _, _, cm2 =
   978→        Ox_gameseg.get_seg_masks
   979→          ~checkp:hit_pnt ~rad:0 ~seg_verts:sv2 ~side_types:st2 ~normals:n2 ~seg_vert_positions:svp2
   980→      in

  1003→        fvi_sub_v2
  1004→          ~collision_table ~n_objects ~is_d2 ~game_time ~ignore_obj_list

  1053→(* sphere_intersects_wall_v2: uses on-demand effects.
  1054→   Takes header: [pnt.xyz, rad, segnum, n_segments] (6 ints). *)
  1055→let sphere_intersects_wall_v2 (arr : int array) =
  1056→  let pnt = arr.(0), arr.(1), arr.(2) in
  1057→  let rad = arr.(3) in
  1058→  let start_segnum = arr.(4) in
  1059→  let n_segments = arr.(5) in
  1060→  let visited = Array.create ~len:n_segments false in
  1061→  let rec walk segnum =
  1062→    if segnum < 0 || segnum >= n_segments then 0
  1063→    else if visited.(segnum) then 0
  1064→    else (
  1065→      visited.(segnum) <- true;
  1066→      let seg_data = Effect.perform (Ox_gameseg.Fetch_segment_data segnum) in
  1067→      let children = Array.init 6 ~f:(fun i -> seg_data.(i)) in
  1068→      let side_types = Array.init 6 ~f:(fun i -> seg_data.(6 + i)) in
  1069→      let seg_verts = Array.init 8 ~f:(fun i -> seg_data.(12 + i)) in
  1070→      let normals =
  1071→        Array.init 12 ~f:(fun i ->
  1072→          let off = 20 + (i * 3) in
  1073→          seg_data.(off), seg_data.(off + 1), seg_data.(off + 2))
  1074→      in
  1075→      let seg_vert_positions =
  1076→        Array.init 8 ~f:(fun i ->
  1077→          let off = 56 + (i * 3) in
  1078→          seg_data.(off), seg_data.(off + 1), seg_data.(off + 2))
  1079→      in
  1080→      let facemask, _sidemask, _centermask =
  1081→        Ox_gameseg.get_seg_masks ~checkp:pnt ~rad ~seg_verts ~side_types ~normals ~seg_vert_positions
  1082→      in
  1083→      if facemask = 0 then 0
  1084→      else (
  1085→        let result = ref 0 in
  1086→        let facebit = ref 1 in
  1087→        let side = ref 0 in
  1088→        while !side < 6 && facemask >= !facebit && !result = 0 do
  1089→          let face = ref 0 in
  1090→          while !face < 2 && !result = 0 do
  1091→            if facemask land !facebit <> 0
  1092→            then (
  1093→              let sn = !side in
  1094→              let fn = !face in
  1095→              let _num_faces, vertex_list =
  1096→                Ox_gameseg.create_abs_vertex_lists
  1097→                  ~side_type:side_types.(sn) ~seg_verts ~sidenum:sn
  1098→              in
  1099→              let nv = if _num_faces = 1 then 4 else 3 in
  1100→              let norm = normals.((sn * 2) + fn) in
  1101→              let vpos abs_idx =
  1102→                Ox_gameseg.lookup_vpos ~seg_verts ~seg_vert_positions ~abs_idx
  1103→              in
  1104→              let face_verts =
  1105→                Array.init nv ~f:(fun i -> vpos vertex_list.((fn * 3) + i))
  1106→              in
  1107→              let face_hit_type =
  1108→                check_sphere_to_face ~pnt ~norm ~nv ~rad ~vertex_positions:face_verts
  1109→              in
  1110→              if face_hit_type <> 0
  1111→              then (
  1112→                let child = children.(sn) in
  1113→                if child <= -1 then result := 1 else if walk child <> 0 then result := 1));
  1114→            facebit := !facebit lsl 1;
  1115→            incr face
  1116→          done;
  1117→          incr side
  1118→        done;
  1119→        !result))
  1120→  in
  1121→  walk start_segnum

  1128→      ~p0 ~startseg ~p1 ~tracker_objnum

# Let's also check the fudged_rad double-adjustment issue more carefully.
# In C fvi_sub:
#   fudged_rad is set to rad, then possibly adjusted based on thisobjnum's type
#   check_vector_to_object(fudged_rad, obj=objnum, otherobj=thisobjnum)
#   Inside check_vector_to_object: size is adjusted based on obj->type (the TARGET)
#   Then uses size+rad where rad=fudged_rad
#
# In OCaml fvi_sub_v2:
#   fudged_rad is set similarly
#   But then check_vector_to_object is called with rad:!fudged_rad AND also passes otherobj_parent_type
#   Inside check_vector_to_object: adjusts size based on obj_type, then does adjustment based on otherobj_type
#   Then calls check_vector_to_sphere_1 with sphere_rad:(!size + rad)
#
# Wait, there's a double adjustment issue! In C:
#   fudged_rad already adjusts for thisobj being a player
#   check_vector_to_object ALSO adjusts size for obj being a player
#   These are different objects being checked

# Let's trace the specific case where thisobj=player, obj=player in C:
# fudged_rad = rad/2 (because thisobj is player, obj is player)
# check_vector_to_object(fudged_rad, obj=player, otherobj=player)
#   -> size (of obj player) /= 2 (because obj is player and otherobj is player)
#   -> check_vector_to_sphere_1(size/2 + rad/2)
# 
# In OCaml:
#   fudged_rad = rad/2 
#   check_vector_to_object(rad=rad/2, obj_size, obj_type=player, otherobj_type=player)
#     -> size /= 2 (because obj_type is player and otherobj_type is player)
#     -> check_vector_to_sphere_1(size/2 + rad/2)
#

(* ── laser_are_related ────────────────────────────────── *)

(* D2 additional weapon IDs *)
let phoenix_id = 34
let guidedmiss_id = 37
let robot_superprox_id = 53
let f1_0 = 0x10000

(* packed: [o1_idx, o1_type, o1_id, o1_sig, o1_parent_num, o1_parent_sig, o1_creation_time,
            o2_idx, o2_type, o2_id, o2_sig, o2_parent_num, o2_parent_sig, o2_creation_time,
            game_time] = 15 ints *)
let laser_are_related_d1 packed =
  let o1_idx = packed.(0) in
  let o1_type = packed.(1) in
  let o1_id = packed.(2) in
  let o1_sig = packed.(3) in
  let o1_pnum = packed.(4) in
  let o1_psig = packed.(5) in
  let o1_ctime = packed.(6) in
  let o2_idx = packed.(7) in
  let o2_type = packed.(8) in
  let o2_id = packed.(9) in
  let o2_sig = packed.(10) in
  let o2_pnum = packed.(11) in
  let o2_psig = packed.(12) in
  let _o2_ctime = packed.(13) in
  let game_time = packed.(14) in
  if o1_idx < 0 || o2_idx < 0
  then 0 (* See if o2 is the parent of o1 *)
  else if o1_type = obj_weapon && o1_pnum = o2_idx && o1_psig = o2_sig
  then
    if o1_id <> proximity_id || o1_ctime + (f1_0 * 2) >= game_time
    then 1
    else 0 (* See if o1 is the parent of o2 *)
  else if o2_type = obj_weapon && o2_pnum = o1_idx && o2_psig = o1_sig
  then 1 (* They must both be weapons *)
  else if o1_type <> obj_weapon || o2_type <> obj_weapon
  then 0 (* Siblings with same parent *)
  else if o1_psig = o2_psig
  then if o1_id = proximity_id || o2_id = proximity_id then 0 else 1
  else 0
;;

let laser_are_related_d2 packed =
  let o1_idx = packed.(0) in
  let o1_type = packed.(1) in
  let o1_id = packed.(2) in
  let _o1_sig = packed.(3) in
  let o1_pnum = packed.(4) in
  let o1_psig = packed.(5) in
  let o1_ctime = packed.(6) in
  let o2_idx = packed.(7) in
  let o2_type = packed.(8) in
  let o2_id = packed.(9) in
  let _o2_sig = packed.(10) in
  let o2_pnum = packed.(11) in
  let o2_psig = packed.(12) in
  let o2_ctime = packed.(13) in
  let game_time = packed.(14) in
  if o1_idx < 0 || o2_idx < 0
  then 0 (* D2 time-based unrelate check for o1-is-weapon, o2-is-parent *)
  else if o1_type = obj_weapon && o1_pnum = o2_idx && o1_psig = packed.(10) (* o2_sig *)
  then
    if
      (o1_id = phoenix_id && game_time > o1_ctime + (f1_0 / 4))
      || (o1_id = guidedmiss_id && game_time > o1_ctime + (f1_0 * 2))
      || ((o1_id = proximity_id || o1_id = superprox_id)
          && game_time > o1_ctime + (f1_0 * 4))
    then 0
    else 1 (* D2 time-based unrelate check for o2-is-weapon, o1-is-parent *)
  else if o2_type = obj_weapon && o2_pnum = o1_idx && o2_psig = packed.(3) (* o1_sig *)
  then
    if
      (o2_id = phoenix_id && game_time > o2_ctime + (f1_0 / 4))
      || (o2_id = guidedmiss_id && game_time > o2_ctime + (f1_0 * 2))
      || ((o2_id = proximity_id || o2_id = superprox_id)
          && game_time > o2_ctime + (f1_0 * 4))
    then 0
    else 1 (* Both must be weapons *)
  else if o1_type <> obj_weapon || o2_type <> obj_weapon
  then 0 (* Siblings with same parent — prox/superprox time check *)
  else if o1_psig = o2_psig
  then
    if
      o1_id = proximity_id
      || o2_id = proximity_id
      || o1_id = superprox_id
      || o2_id = superprox_id
    then
      if game_time > o1_ctime + (f1_0 / 2) || game_time > o2_ctime + (f1_0 / 2)
      then 0
      else 1
    else 1 (* Mine/prox/robot-superprox: never related *)
  else if
    o1_id = robot_superprox_id
    || o2_id = robot_superprox_id
    || o1_id = proximity_id
    || o2_id = proximity_id
    || o1_id = superprox_id
    || o2_id = superprox_id
    || o1_id = pmine_id
    || o2_id = pmine_id
  then 0
  else 1
;;

# laser_are_related: both OCaml and C return 0 if o1<0 or o2<0 (thisobjnum=-1 case).
# So when thisobjnum=-1 and thisobj_data=None, OCaml skips the check but C also returns 0.
# That means the net effect is the same: no skip from laser_are_related when thisobjnum=-1.
# OCaml correctly skips the call (same result, just no-op).

# Now, let's look at the critical segment list handling in the C seglist copy bug:
# C fvi_sub line ~1151-1155:
#   for (ii=0; i<temp_n_segs && *n_segs<MAX_FVI_SEGS-1;)
#     seglist[(*n_segs)++] = temp_seglist[ii++];
# Bug: condition uses `i` (n_segs_visited loop counter) not `ii`.
# After segs_visited search loop: `i` equals `n_segs_visited` (loop ran to completion).
# So condition is: n_segs_visited < temp_n_segs
# If n_segs_visited < temp_n_segs, all elements get copied (ii increments properly in body).
# If n_segs_visited >= temp_n_segs, zero elements are copied.
# 
# This is different from OCaml's correct `ii < temp_n_segs`.
# But this only matters when FQ_GET_SEGLIST is set AND the player object is triggering physics.

# Physics.cpp uses FQ_GET_SEGLIST only for OBJ_PLAYER.
# So for the player object's physics, the seglist may differ between C and OCaml.
# This affects which segment the player is considered to be in after crossing a boundary.
# This could affect hit_seg determination in find_vector_intersection.

# Let's also check the `find_vector_intersection` top-level to see how hit_seg is resolved
# from the seglist in C vs OCaml.

# C top-level resolution:
# 1. After fvi_sub: hit_seg2 is returned as *ints from fvi_sub
# 2. if (hit_seg2!=-1 && !get_seg_masks(...).centermask) hit_seg = hit_seg2
# 3. else hit_seg = find_point_seg(hit_pnt, startseg)
# 4. if (HIT_WALL && hit_seg==-1 && fvi_hit_seg2!=-1 && centermask==0) hit_seg = fvi_hit_seg2

# OCaml top-level resolution looks similar. Let's verify...
# Already read this at line 972-1009 in ox_fvi.ml. 

# Now, one more thing to check: the hit_seg copy from sub_hit_seg in fvi_sub.
# C: if (sub_hit_seg!=-1) hit_seg = sub_hit_seg;
# OCaml: if sub_hit_seg <> -1 then hit_seg := sub_hit_seg;
# Same. Good.

# Let's also look at the `check_sphere_to_face` call in `sphere_intersects_wall`:
# OCaml sphere_intersects_wall_v2:
#   let face_verts = Array.init nv (fun i -> vpos vertex_list.((fn * 3) + i))
#   let face_hit_type = check_sphere_to_face ~pnt ~norm ~nv ~rad ~vertex_positions:face_verts
# C sphere_intersects_wall:
#   face_hit_type = check_sphere_to_face(pnt, &seg->sides[side], face, nv, rad, vertex_list)
# C's check_sphere_to_face uses vertex_list[facenum*3+edge] inside check_point_to_face.
# OCaml's face_verts already has facenum*3+i mapped to positions.
# This is equivalent.

# Let's also check the face loop in sphere_intersects_wall_v2:
# OCaml line 1087-1117: iterates face from 0 to 1 (inner loop), side 0 to 5 (outer)
# C: for (side=0,bit=1; side<6; side++) for (face=0; face<2; face++, bit<<=1)
# Same order.

# But! In OCaml sphere_intersects_wall_v2 (lines 1088-1117):
# while !side < 6 && facemask >= !facebit && !result = 0 do
#   let face = ref 0 in
#   while !face < 2 && !result = 0 do
#     if facemask land !facebit <> 0 then (
#       ...
#       if face_hit_type <> 0 then (
#         let child = children.(sn) in
#         if child <= -1 then result := 1 else if walk child <> 0 then result := 1));
#     facebit := !facebit lsl 1;
#     incr face
#   done;
#   incr side
# done;
# 
# Notice: `facebit` is incremented whether or not `facemask land !facebit <> 0`!
# But the outer loop condition is `facemask >= !facebit` so it terminates when facebit > facemask.
# C also increments bit unconditionally: for (face=0;face<2;face++,bit<<=1)
# Same behavior.

# But wait! Look at the early exit condition more carefully:
# OCaml inner loop: `while !face < 2 && !result = 0 do`
# If result becomes 1 INSIDE the inner loop (face=0), the inner loop exits early (face stays 0).
# Then `facebit := !facebit lsl 1` and `incr face` are STILL executed (they're after the while).
# Wait, no - the `facebit` increment is INSIDE the inner while loop body:
# while !face < 2 && !result = 0 do
#   if facemask land !facebit <> 0 then ( ... set result );
#   facebit := !facebit lsl 1;
#   incr face
# done;
# 
# If result=1 is set during face=0, the NEXT iteration check `!result = 0` fails, loop exits.
# So facebit was shifted once (face=0 iteration), but face=1 iteration doesn't happen.
# facebit has been shifted once. This could leave facebit in the wrong state for the next side.
# 
# But since result=1 exits everything (outer loop also exits), this doesn't matter.

# In OCaml: bit shifts at END of for-body, which is also after each iteration. Same.

# HOWEVER there's a subtle difference in the for-loop structure!
# OCaml: `for face = 0 to 1 do ... bit := !bit lsl 1 done`
# This always executes both iterations (face 0 and face 1), even if `!quit` is set.

content.text:
Here is a detailed comparison of all significant findings between the C `fvi_sub` / `find_vector_intersection` and the OCaml `fvi_sub_v2` / `find_vector_intersection_v2`.

---

## Findings

### 1. Seglist copy bug — C has a typo that OCaml does not replicate

**C `fvi_sub`, inside the `if (sub_hit_type != HIT_NONE)` and `if (d < closest_d)` branch:**

```c
if (flags&FQ_GET_SEGLIST) {
    int ii;
    for (ii=0; i<temp_n_segs && *n_segs<MAX_FVI_SEGS-1;)
        seglist[(*n_segs)++] = temp_seglist[ii++];
}
```

The loop condition uses `i` (the outer `segs_visited` search counter, which equals `n_segs_visited` at that point) instead of `ii`. This is a C bug: the copy only happens at all if `n_segs_visited < temp_n_segs`, and iterates correctly (since `ii` increments in the body). OCaml's version is the correct `while !ii < temp_n_segs ...` using `ii` throughout.

**Impact:** This only triggers when `FQ_GET_SEGLIST` is set. In `physics.cpp`, that flag is only used for `OBJ_PLAYER`. The buggy C behavior means the player's traversed-segment list in the recursive crossing case is often wrong (undercopied). OCaml produces a different (correct) seglist here. Whether this causes a collision result divergence depends on whether the seglist is used to resolve `hit_seg` after the fact — which it is in `find_vector_intersection`'s top-level logic. The `hit_seg` resolved from the seglist is what gets written to `hit_data->hit_seg`, which physics then uses to update `obj->segnum`. So this is a real potential source of divergence for the player object.

---

### 2. OCaml's `is_d2` guard on the FQ_IGNORE_POWERUPS check

**C `fvi_sub` (D2):**
```c
if (Objects[objnum].type == OBJ_POWERUP)
    if (flags & FQ_IGNORE_POWERUPS)
        continue;
```
No is_d2 guard.

**OCaml `fvi_sub_v2`:**
```ocaml
if (not !skip) && is_d2 && obj_type_ob = obj_powerup && flags land fq_ignore_powerups <> 0
then skip := true
```

The `is_d2` guard in OCaml means that in D1 mode, powerups are never skipped even if `fq_ignore_powerups` is set. However, `FQ_IGNORE_POWERUPS` is only defined in `main_d2/fvi.h` and is only set by D2 callers (`laser.cpp` D2 weapon fire checks). D1 code never sets this flag, so this difference is harmless in practice. For D2, it matches C exactly.

---

### 3. `check_sphere_to_face` — the `checkp` vs `pnt` discrepancy (non-issue)

In C, `check_sphere_to_face` takes `pnt`, copies it to `checkp = *pnt`, then passes `pnt` (the original) to `check_point_to_face` for the edgemask, but uses `checkp` for the edge distance computation. Since `checkp = *pnt`, these are always identical values. OCaml uses `pnt` for both paths. No behavioral difference.

---

### 4. `check_line_to_face` — `face_hit_type` values returned

`check_line_to_face` can return `IT_FACE` (1), `IT_EDGE` (2), or `IT_NONE` (0). `IT_POINT` (3) is never returned by `check_sphere_to_face` (it returns `IT_NONE` when `itype == IT_POINT && dist <= rad`). So `face_hit_type` in `fvi_sub` is 0, 1, or 2. The check `if (face_hit_type)` in C and `if face_hit_type <> 0` in OCaml are both correct for any nonzero value. No difference here.

---

### 5. `special_check_line_to_face` — vertex list construction differs subtly between C and OCaml

**C:** `check_point_to_face(p0, s, facenum, nv, vertex_list)` where `vertex_list` is the full 6-element list from `create_abs_vertex_lists`. Inside `check_point_to_face`, it reads `vertex_list[facenum*3+edge]` and calls `Vertices[...]` directly.

**OCaml:** `face_verts = Array.init nv (fun i -> vpos vertex_list.((facenum * 3) + i))` then passes `face_verts` (already-resolved positions) to `check_point_to_face`. Same data, different packaging. No divergence.

The edge vertices in `special_check_line_to_face`:
- C: `edge_v0 = &Vertices[vertex_list[facenum*3+edgenum]]`
- OCaml: `edge_v0 = face_verts.(!edgenum)` where `face_verts.(i) = vpos vertex_list.((facenum * 3) + i)`

These are identical.

---

### 6. Robot-robot collision skip logic — correct for both D1 and D2

**C D2:** Always skips robot-robot (no attack_type condition; the original condition is commented out).
**C D1:** Skips if either robot's `attack_type == 0`.

**OCaml:**
```ocaml
if thisobj_type = obj_robot && obj_type_ob = obj_robot then
  if is_d2 then skip := true
  else if not (attack_type_ob <> 0 && thisobj_attack <> 0) then skip := true
```

This correctly matches both D1 and D2 behavior.

---

### 7. `sphere_intersects_wall` — OCaml uses local bool array, C uses global `segs_visited`

C `sphere_intersects_wall` adds the current `segnum` to the global `segs_visited[n_segs_visited++]` before checking faces, then scans this global list when recursing into children. `object_intersects_wall` resets `n_segs_visited = 0` before calling.

OCaml `sphere_intersects_wall_v2` uses a local `visited : bool array` of size `n_segments`. Behavior is equivalent: both mark on entry, both check before recursing.

---

### 8. `find_vector_intersection` top-level hit_seg resolution

C after `fvi_sub`:
```c
if (hit_seg2!=-1 && !get_seg_masks(&hit_pnt,hit_seg2,0).centermask)
    hit_seg = hit_seg2;
else
    hit_seg = find_point_seg(&hit_pnt, fq->startseg);
```

OCaml:
```ocaml
if hit_seg2 <> -1 then (
  let sd2 = Effect.perform ... in
  let _, _, cm2 = get_seg_masks ~checkp:hit_pnt ~rad:0 ... in
  if cm2 = 0 then hit_seg := hit_seg2);
if !hit_seg = -1 then
  hit_seg := find_point_seg_fvi_v2 ~point:hit_pnt ~segnum:startseg ~n_segments;
```

These are logically equivalent. The OCaml uses `find_point_seg_fvi_v2` which is the OCaml port of `find_point_seg`. The subsequent retry with `rad:0` and the `fvi_hit_seg2` HACK also both appear in both versions.

---

### Summary of significant differences

The only real difference that can affect collision results is:

**Bug 1 (seglist copy, `i` vs `ii`):** Only affects player physics (`FQ_GET_SEGLIST`). OCaml correctly copies the sub-segment traversal list; C copies it incorrectly (copies 0 entries if `n_segs_visited >= temp_n_segs`, otherwise copies all). In a typical traversal through 1-2 segments, `n_segs_visited` is small and `temp_n_segs` is likely small too. The resulting seglist difference could cause `hit_seg` to resolve differently, making the player end up in a different segment number after crossing a boundary — which would produce a divergence in the state log for `segnum` offset 14 in the object struct.

Everything else — `face_hit_type` semantics, `check_sphere_to_face`, `check_line_to_face` plane-point selection, wall traversal, object skip logic, radius adjustments, the `HIT_BAD_P0` check — matches the C behavior correctly.

OCaml's version is the correct `while !ii < temp_n_segs ...` using `ii` throughout.

**Impact:** This only triggers when `FQ_GET_SEGLIST` is set. In `physics.cpp`, that flag is only used for `OBJ_PLAYER`. The buggy C behavior means the player's traversed-segment list in the recursive crossing case is often wrong (undercopied). OCaml produces a different (correct) seglist here. Whether this causes a collision result divergence depends on whether the seglist is used to resolve `hit_seg` after the fact — which it is in `find_vector_intersection`'s top-level logic. The `hit_seg` resolved from the seglist is what gets written to `hit_data->hit_seg`, which physics then uses to update `obj->segnum`. So this is a real potential source of divergence for the player object.

---

### 2. OCaml's `is_d2` guard on the FQ_IGNORE_POWERUPS check

**C `fvi_sub` (D2):**
```c
if (Objects[objnum].type == OBJ_POWERUP)
    if (flags & FQ_IGNORE_POWERUPS)
        continue;
```
No is_d2 guard.

**OCaml `fvi_sub_v2`:**
```ocaml
if (not !skip) && is_d2 && obj_type_ob = obj_powerup && flags land fq_ignore_powerups <> 0
then skip := true
```

The `is_d2` guard in OCaml means that in D1 mode, powerups are never skipped even if `fq_ignore_powerups` is set. However, `FQ_IGNORE_POWERUPS` is only defined in `main_d2/fvi.h` and is only set by D2 callers (`laser.cpp` D2 weapon fire checks). D1 code never sets this flag, so this difference is harmless in practice. For D2, it matches C exactly.

---

### 3. `check_sphere_to_face` — the `checkp` vs `pnt` discrepancy (non-issue)

In C, `check_sphere_to_face` takes `pnt`, copies it to `checkp = *pnt`, then passes `pnt` (the original) to `check_point_to_face` for the edgemask, but uses `checkp` for the edge distance computation. Since `checkp = *pnt`, these are always identical values. OCaml uses `pnt` for both paths. No behavioral difference.

---

### 4. `check_line_to_face` — `face_hit_type` values returned

`check_line_to_face` can return `IT_FACE` (1), `IT_EDGE` (2), or `IT_NONE` (0). `IT_POINT` (3) is never returned by `check_sphere_to_face` (it returns `IT_NONE` when `itype == IT_POINT && dist <= rad`). So `face_hit_type` in `fvi_sub` is 0, 1, or 2. The check `if (face_hit_type)` in C and `if face_hit_type <> 0` in OCaml are both correct for any nonzero value. No difference here.

---

### 5. `special_check_line_to_face` — vertex list construction differs subtly between C and OCaml

**C:** `check_point_to_face(p0, s, facenum, nv, vertex_list)` where `vertex_list` is the full 6-element list from `create_abs_vertex_lists`. Inside `check_point_to_face`, it reads `vertex_list[facenum*3+edge]` and calls `Vertices[...]` directly.

**OCaml:** `face_verts = Array.init nv (fun i -> vpos vertex_list.((facenum * 3) + i))` then passes `face_verts` (already-resolved positions) to `check_point_to_face`. Same data, different packaging. No divergence.

The edge vertices in `special_check_line_to_face`:
- C: `edge_v0 = &Vertices[vertex_list[facenum*3+edgenum]]`
- OCaml: `edge_v0 = face_verts.(!edgenum)` where `face_verts.(i) = vpos vertex_list.((facenum * 3) + i)`

These are identical.

---

### 6. Robot-robot collision skip logic — correct for both D1 and D2

**C D2:** Always skips robot-robot (no attack_type condition; the original condition is commented out).
**C D1:** Skips if either robot's `attack_type == 0`.

**OCaml:**
```ocaml
if thisobj_type = obj_robot && obj_type_ob = obj_robot then
  if is_d2 then skip := true
  else if not (attack_type_ob <> 0 && thisobj_attack <> 0) then skip := true
```

This correctly matches both D1 and D2 behavior.

---

### 7. `sphere_intersects_wall` — OCaml uses local bool array, C uses global `segs_visited`

C `sphere_intersects_wall` adds the current `segnum` to the global `segs_visited[n_segs_visited++]` before checking faces, then scans this global list when recursing into children. `object_intersects_wall` resets `n_segs_visited = 0` before calling.

OCaml `sphere_intersects_wall_v2` uses a local `visited : bool array` of size `n_segments`. Behavior is equivalent: both mark on entry, both check before recursing.

---

### 8. `find_vector_intersection` top-level hit_seg resolution

C after `fvi_sub`:
```c
if (hit_seg2!=-1 && !get_seg_masks(&hit_pnt,hit_seg2,0).centermask)
    hit_seg = hit_seg2;
else
    hit_seg = find_point_seg(&hit_pnt, fq->startseg);
```

OCaml:
```ocaml
if hit_seg2 <> -1 then (
  let sd2 = Effect.perform ... in
  let _, _, cm2 = get_seg_masks ~checkp:hit_pnt ~rad:0 ... in
  if cm2 = 0 then hit_seg := hit_seg2);
if !hit_seg = -1 then
  hit_seg := find_point_seg_fvi_v2 ~point:hit_pnt ~segnum:startseg ~n_segments;
```

These are logically equivalent. The OCaml uses `find_point_seg_fvi_v2` which is the OCaml port of `find_point_seg`. The subsequent retry with `rad:0` and the `fvi_hit_seg2` HACK also both appear in both versions.

---

### Summary of significant differences

The only real difference that can affect collision results is:

**Bug 1 (seglist copy, `i` vs `ii`):** Only affects player physics (`FQ_GET_SEGLIST`). OCaml correctly copies the sub-segment traversal list; C copies it incorrectly (copies 0 entries if `n_segs_visited >= temp_n_segs`, otherwise copies all). In a typical traversal through 1-2 segments, `n_segs_visited` is small and `temp_n_segs` is likely small too. The resulting seglist difference could cause `hit_seg` to resolve differently, making the player end up in a different segment number after crossing a boundary — which would produce a divergence in the state log for `segnum` offset 14 in the object struct.

Everything else — `face_hit_type` semantics, `check_sphere_to_face`, `check_line_to_face` plane-point selection, wall traversal, object skip logic, radius adjustments, the `HIT_BAD_P0` check — matches the C behavior correctly.

content.thinking:
OK, so `vm_vec_add` and `vm_vec_sub` both use `wrap_add_i32` for 32-bit wrapping. Good.

But there's a subtle issue with `vm_vec_sub`: it uses `-bx` to negate, then `wrap_add_i32`. In C, subtraction `a - b` for 32-bit ints: if `a = 0` and `b = INT32_MIN = -2147483648`, then C computes `0 - (-2147483648) = 2147483648` which overflows to `-2147483648` in 32-bit. 

In OCaml, the negation `-bx` produces a 63-bit value, so when `bx = -2147483648`, negating it gives `2147483648`. Then `wrap_add_i32` adds this to the first operand and wraps the result back to 32-bit, which produces the same overflow behavior as C.

file.content:
let find_plane_line_intersection ~plane_pnt ~plane_norm ~p0 ~p1 ~rad =
  let open Ox_math in
  let d = vm_vec_sub ~a:p1 ~b:p0 in
  let w = vm_vec_sub ~a:p0 ~b:plane_pnt in
  let num_raw = vm_vec_dotprod ~a:plane_norm ~b:w in
  let den = wrap32 (-vm_vec_dotprod ~a:plane_norm ~b:d) in
  let num = wrap32 (num_raw - rad) in
  (* Check for various bad values — C operator precedence:
     (den == 0) || ((den > 0) && ((num > den) || ((-num >> 15) >= den)))
                 || (den < 0 && num < den) *)
  let neg_num = wrap32 (-num) in
  if den = 0
  then None
  else if den > 0 && (num > den || neg_num asr 15 >= den)
  then None
  else if den < 0 && num < den
  then None
  else (
    let f1_0 = 0x10000 in
    if abs num / (f1_0 / 2) >= abs den
    then None (* would overflow *)
    else (
      let k = fixdiv ~a:num ~b:den in
      let dx, dy, dz = d in
      if oflow_check ~a:dx ~b:k || oflow_check ~a:dy ~b:k || oflow_check ~a:dz ~b:k
      then None
      else (
        let scaled = vm_vec_scale2 ~v:d ~n:num ~d:den in
        let newp = vm_vec_add ~a:p0 ~b:scaled in
        Some (k, newp))))
;;

(* 2D point-in-polygon test. Projects face onto a 2D plane using ij_table
   based on the largest component of the normal. Returns an edgemask where
   bit N is set if the point is outside edge N. *)
let check_point_to_face ~checkp ~norm ~nv ~(vertex_positions : vec3 array) =
  let open Ox_math in
  let cpx, cpy, cpz = checkp in
  let cp = [| cpx; cpy; cpz |] in
  let nx, ny, nz = norm in

~vertex_list ~seg_verts ~seg_vert_positions

            then (

                let base_wid = wid.(sn) in
                if thisobjnum = player_objnum && physics_cheat

                  if children.(sn) >= 0 then w lor wid_fly_flag else w)
                else base_wid
              in
              let is_flyable = wid_flag land wid_fly_flag <> 0 in
              let is_trans =
                if is_d2
                then
                  wid_flag land wid_render_flag <> 0
                  && wid_flag land wid_rendpast_flag <> 0
                else wid_flag = wid_transparent_wall_d1
              in
              let passable =
                is_flyable
                || (is_trans && flags land fq_transwall <> 0)
                || (is_trans
                    && flags land fq_transpoint <> 0
                    &&
                    let hx, hy, hz = hit_point in
                    !check_trans_wall_callback startseg sn face hx hy hz <> 0)
              in
              if passable
              then (
                let newsegnum = children.(sn) in
                let already_visited = ref false in
                for i = 0 to st.n_segs_visited - 1 do
                  if st.segs_visited.(i) = newsegnum then already_visited := true
                done;

OK I notice something potentially interesting. In the C FVI code, WALL_IS_DOORWAY is computed using the segment data, and can include various flags like WID_FLY_FLAG (1), WID_RENDER_FLAG (2), WID_RENDPAST_FLAG (4), etc.

The OCaml code accesses `wid.(sn)` which is `sd.(80 + sn)` from the segment data. This is the pre-computed `WALL_IS_DOORWAY(seg, i)` value. So it should match C.

Let me look at `check_trans_wall` since it's used for FQ_TRANSPOINT checks. This is used by weapons.Actually, I'm going in circles. 

Let me shift gears and focus on porting functions that would actually move the needle. Looking at the checklist, most of the commonly-called functions are already ported, but I notice `ai_do_cloak_stuff` is still unported—it's trivial to implement but it modifies C globals that aren't tracked in the state log.

The other candidates like `add_awareness_event`, `create_awareness_event`, and `process_awareness_events` all modify the awareness queue and `Overall_agitation`, which is a tracked global. `create_awareness_event` in particular calls `add_awareness_event`, uses `P_Rand()`, and increments `Overall_agitation`—all C state modifications that get invoked from collision handlers and other places throughout the codebase.

But here's the issue: `create_awareness_event` is called via an effect from the OCaml collision handlers. If I port it to pure OCaml, I'd need to maintain the entire awareness event queue in OCaml too, which gets complicated. Let me look at something simpler instead—maybe `collide_player_and_wall` or `collide_weapon_and_wall` since those are called directly from the OCaml physics simulation. Actually, let me just pick something straightforward like `ai_do_cloak_stuff` and port it, since it's just copying the player position to all AI entities.

Wait, but that function modifies `Believed_player_pos` and `Believed_player_seg`, which are already used by the ported `do_ai_frame` function. They're passed in from C right now, not modified within OCaml itself. Let me look at `Laser_do_weapon_sequence` instead—the homing weapon part is already ported, but the rest handles weapon animation and could be affecting object divergences.

Actually, `Laser_do_weapon_sequence` runs in C for both builds since it's called from the game loop for each weapon object each frame, so porting it wouldn't reduce divergences between the two builds. The real problem is that divergences come from different execution paths—ported functions run in OCaml in one build but in C in the other. Porting more functions to OCaml won't change what the C-ref build does, so the divergences persist. I need to either fix bugs in the OCaml code itself or figure out why the already-ported functions are producing different results.

The OCaml `do_physics_sim` calls an OCaml version of the vector intersection function through effects, which itself uses effects to fetch segment data from C, whereas the C-ref build calls the C implementation directly. If the OCaml port has bugs, that would explain the divergences. There's also the possibility that the two builds are calling the random number generator at different times, which could throw off the entire sequence of results.

Looking at the code, `create_awareness_event` definitely calls `P_Rand()`, and `compute_vis_and_vec` probably does too for sound effects. Since the OCaml build invokes `d_rand()` through effects at different points in the per-robot loop than the C-ref build does, the random state diverges and cascades into different behavior downstream.

The 657k divergences are really just a consequence of how FVI differences ripple through the code paths and change when `d_rand()` gets called. Rather than chase those down, I should focus on porting more functions to make actual progress, even if they don't directly shrink the divergence count.

Looking at what's left to port, `collide_robot_and_wall` seems like the most straightforward — it's mainly just checking collision conditions.

But wait, that function calls `wall_open_door()` which is a C effect, and it also needs access to wall data, robot info, and segment data. To port it properly in OCaml, I'd need new effects to query that information. The constraint is that I can't modify the bridge files or the main AI code, so I can't add those effects.

Looking at what I can actually port: functions that work with data already exposed through existing effects and use pure OCaml logic. Let me check what's currently available through the effect system...

Actually, rereading the rules—I can add new OCaml files and modify existing ox_*.ml files freely. The restriction is only on the bridge layer itself. So the question becomes: what data can I access through the existing effects?

I have segment data, object data, AI state, player data, robot_info, and collision data all already passed into do_ai_frame. That's actually a lot to work with for porting functions that don't need new external data sources.

    21→external fetch_segment_data_c : int -> int array = "cd_ox_fetch_segment_data"
    22→external fetch_object_data_c : int -> int array = "cd_ox_fetch_object_data"
    23→external fetch_collision_table_c : unit -> int array = "cd_ox_fetch_collision_table"
    24→
    25→(* Run an OCaml function under effect handlers for FVI data fetching.
    26→   Handles: Fetch_segment_data, Fetch_object_data, Fetch_collision_table *)
    27→let run_with_fvi_effects : 'a. (unit -> 'a) -> 'a =
    28→  fun f ->
    29→  Effect.Deep.match_with f ()
    30→    { retc = (fun x -> x)
    31→    ; exnc = (fun e -> raise e)
    32→    ; effc = (fun (type a) (eff : a Effect.t) ->
    33→        match eff with
    34→        | Ox_gameseg.Fetch_segment_data segnum ->
    35→          Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
    36→            let data = fetch_segment_data_c segnum in
    37→            Effect.Deep.continue k data)
    38→        | Ox_fvi.Fetch_object_data objnum ->
    39→          Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
    40→            let data = fetch_object_data_c objnum in
    41→            Effect.Deep.continue k data)
    42→        | Ox_fvi.Fetch_collision_table ->
    43→          Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
    44→            let data = fetch_collision_table_c () in
    45→            Effect.Deep.continue k data)
    46→        | _ -> None)

file.content:
let check_line_to_face
      ~p0
      ~p1
      ~norm
      ~rad
      ~facenum
      ~nv
      ~num_faces
      ~(vertex_list : int array)
      ~(seg_verts : int array)
      ~(seg_vert_positions : vec3 array)
  =
  let open Ox_math in
  let vpos abs_idx = Ox_gameseg.lookup_vpos ~seg_verts ~seg_vert_positions ~abs_idx in
  (* Use lowest point number as plane point *)
  let vertnum =
    if num_faces = 2
    then min vertex_list.(0) vertex_list.(2)
    else (
      let v = ref vertex_list.(0) in
      for i = 1 to 3 do
        if vertex_list.(i) < !v then v := vertex_list.(i)
      done;
      !v)
  in
  let plane_pnt = vpos vertnum in
  match find_plane_line_intersection ~plane_pnt ~plane_norm:norm ~p0 ~p1 ~rad with
  | None -> it_none, (0, 0, 0)
  | Some (_k, newp) ->
    let checkp =
      if rad <> 0 then vm_vec_scale_add2 ~dest:newp ~src:norm ~k:(-rad) else newp
    in
    (* Build vertex positions for this face *)
    let face_verts = Array.init nv ~f:(fun i -> vpos vertex_list.((facenum * 3) + i)) in
    let hit =
      check_sphere_to_face ~pnt:checkp ~norm ~nv ~rad ~vertex_positions:face_verts
    in
    hit, newp
;;

(* special_check_line_to_face: handles the case where both start and end
   positions poke through the plane of a side. *)
let special_check_line_to_face
      ~p0
      ~p1

content.thinking:
OK, `lookup_vpos` finds the vertex index in `seg_verts` (segment's vertex indices) and returns the corresponding position from `seg_vert_positions`. The data comes from the `Fetch_segment_data` effect which packs:
- `out[12+i]` = `seg->verts[i]` (8 vertex indices)
- `out[56+i*3+0..2]` = Vertices[seg->verts[i]] (8 vertex positions)

So `seg_verts[i]` = absolute vertex index, and `seg_vert_positions[i]` = position of that vertex. Then `lookup_vpos` scans `seg_verts` to find which local index has the matching absolute vertex index, and returns the corresponding position.


In `check_line_to_face`, the `vertex_list` comes from `create_abs_vertex_lists` and contains the absolute vertex indices forming the face. The `vertnum` is calculated as the minimum of those indices, and when I call `lookup_vpos` with it, I'm searching for whether that vertex exists in the current segment — but I'm realizing there's a potential issue if the vertex isn't actually part of this segment.

Actually, wait — each segment has exactly 8 vertices, and the faces are built from those same 8 vertices, so `vertnum` should always be found. Let me stop chasing this FVI logic and look for a simpler bug elsewhere in the OCaml code instead.

The loop condition uses `i` instead of `ii`. `i` is the outer loop variable (used for the `segs_visited` check at line 1126). At this point in the code, `i == n_segs_visited` (since the for loop from line 1126 iterated until `i == n_segs_visited`).

So the C code's seglist copy works like this:- If `n_segs_visited < temp_n_segs`, the copy runs and copies `temp_n_segs - n_segs_visited` entries (since `ii` increments but `i` doesn't change)- Actually wait, the loop condition is `i<temp_n_segs` and `i` doesn't change in the loop body. And `ii` starts at 0 and increments. So:  - If `i >=

Analyzing the loop logic more carefully. The condition checks `i < temp_n_segs` where `i` is frozen at `n_segs_visited` from the outer loop, while `ii` increments from 0. If `i >= temp_n_segs`, nothing copies. If `i < temp_n_segs`, the loop runs indefinitely copying entries until hitting the `MAX_FVI_SEGS` limit, since `i` never changes and stays below `temp_n_segs`. So even though the buffer itself won't overflow, the loop copies uninitialized or stale data beyond the actual valid segment count, whereas the OCaml version correctly stops at the boundary by checking the segment index against the actual count.

This difference in seglist contents could affect how `hit_seg` gets resolved during physics calculations, potentially changing the player's segment number and cascading into different physics behavior. file.content:
!hit_type, intp, ints, seglist, !n_segs)
;;

(* Unpack segment data for centermask check (returns just the fields needed) *)
let unpack_seg_for_masks seg_data =
  let seg_verts = Array.init 8 ~f:(fun i -> seg_data.(12 + i)) in
  let side_types = Array.init 6 ~f:(fun i -> seg_data.(6 + i)) in
  let normals =
    Array.init 12 ~f:(fun i ->
      let off = 20 + (i * 3) in
      seg_data.(off), seg_data.(off + 1), seg_data.(off + 2))
  in
  let seg_vert_positions =
    Array.init 8 ~f:(fun i ->
      let off = 56 + (i * 3) in
      seg_data.(off), seg_data.(off + 1), seg_data.(off + 2))
  in
  seg_verts, side_types, normals, seg_vert_positions
;;

(* find_point_seg_fvi_v2: uses Fetch_segment_data effects *)
let find_point_seg_fvi_v2 ~point:(px, py, pz) ~segnum ~n_segments =
  let fetch s =
    let seg_data = Effect.perform (Ox_gameseg.Fetch_segment_data s) in
    let children = Array.init 6 ~f:(fun i -> seg_data.(i)) in
    let side_types = Array.init 6 ~f:(fun i -> seg_data.(6 + i)) in
    let seg_verts = Array.init 8 ~f:(fun i -> seg_data.(12 + i)) in
    let normals =
      Array.init 12 ~f:(fun i ->
        let off = 20 + (i * 3) in
        seg_data.(off), seg_data.(off + 1), seg_data.(off + 2))
    in
    let seg_vert_positions =
      Array.init 8 ~f:(fun i ->
        let off = 56 + (i * 3) in
        seg_data.(off), seg_data.(off + 1), seg_data.(off + 2))
    in
    children, side_types, seg_verts, normals, seg_vert_positions
  in
  let rec trace s iterations =
    if s < 0 || s >= n_segments then -1
    else if iterations > 1024 then s
    else (
      let children, side_types, seg_verts, normals, seg_vert_positions = fetch s in
      let centermask, side_dists =
        Ox_gameseg.get_side_dists
          ~checkp:(px, py, pz) ~seg_verts ~side_types ~normals ~seg_vert_positions
      in
      if centermask = 0 then s
      else (
        let biggest_val = ref 0 in
        let sidenum = ref (-1) in
        for i = 0 to 5 do
          if centermask land (1 lsl i) <> 0 && children.(i) >= 0
          then
            if side_dists.(i) < !biggest_val
            then (
              biggest_val := side_dists.(i);
              sidenum := i)
        done;
        if !sidenum <> -1
        then trace children.(!sidenum) (iterations + 1)
        else -1))
  in
  if segnum < 0 || segnum >= n_segments then -1 else trace segnum 0
;;

(* find_vector_intersection_v2: top-level FVI using on-demand effects.
   Takes just the header array (18+N ints), fetches data via effects. *)
let find_vector_intersection_v2 (arr : int array) =
  let p0 = arr.(h_p0x), arr.(h_p0y), arr.(h_p0z) in
  let startseg = arr.(h_startseg) in
  let p1 = arr.(h_p1x), arr.(h_p1y), arr.(h_p1z) in
  let rad = arr.(h_rad) in
  let thisobjnum = arr.(h_thisobjnum) in
  let flags = arr.(h_flags) in
  let n_segments = arr.(h_n_segments) in
  let n_objects = arr.(h_n_objects) in
  let player_objnum = arr.(h_player_objnum) in
  let physics_cheat = arr.(h_physics_cheat) <> 0 in
  let game_mode_coop = arr.(h_game_mode_coop) <> 0 in
  let game_time = arr.(h_game_time) in
  let is_d2 = arr.(h_is_d2) <> 0 in
  let ignore_count = arr.(h_ignore_obj_count) in
  let ignore_obj_list = Array.init ignore_count ~f:(fun i -> arr.(h_ignore_obj_list + i)) in
  let collision_table = Effect.perform Fetch_collision_table in
  (* Validate startseg *)
  let start_seg_data = Effect.perform (Ox_gameseg.Fetch_segment_data startseg) in
  let seg_verts, side_types, normals, seg_vert_positions =
    unpack_seg_for_masks start_seg_data
  in
  let _, _, centermask =
    Ox_gameseg.get_seg_masks
      ~checkp:p0 ~rad:0 ~seg_verts ~side_types ~normals ~seg_vert_positions
  in
  if centermask <> 0
  then (
    let p0x, p0y, p0z = p0 in
    [| hit_bad_p0; p0x; p0y; p0z; startseg; 0; -1; 0; 0; 0; 0; 0 |])
  else (

content.thinking:
OK, I see the flow for the OCaml build:
1. `find_vector_intersection` (C++ in fvi.cpp) is called2. It packs header, calls `cd_ox_find_vector_intersection_v2`3. OCaml's `find_vector_intersection_v2` runs
4. Results are unpacked back into C structsFor the C-ref build:
1. `find_vector_intersection` falls through to the C path (line 856+)Both should produce the same results if the OCaml FVI matches C FVI exactly.Let me look at the C path's top-level logic to compare with OCaml's top-level logic.

Let me compare the top-level hit_seg resolution logic:

**C:**
1. Call `fvi_sub`, get `hit_seg2`
2. If `hit_seg2 != -1` and `get_seg_masks(hit_pnt, hit_seg2, 0).centermask == 0`, then `hit_seg = hit_seg2`
3. Else `hit_seg = find_point_seg(hit_pnt, startseg)`

**OCaml:**
1. Call `fvi_sub_v2`, get `hit_seg2`


2. If `hit_seg2 != -1`, fetch segment data and check the centermask value
3. When centermask is 0, set `hit_seg = hit_seg2`; otherwise fall back to `find_point_seg_fvi_v2`

There's a subtle difference in how the fallback is structured—the C version uses an else clause, while the OCaml version checks if `hit_seg` is still -1 before calling the fallback function. I'm tracing through the logic more carefully now. When `hit_seg2 != -1` and `centermask != 0`, the C code calls `find_point_seg` in the else branch, but in OCaml `hit_seg` would remain -1 and trigger the second check, also calling `find_point_seg_fvi_v2`. So the behavior matches in this case too.

Now I'm looking at the retry logic when `hit_seg == -1` — the C code calls `fvi_sub` with `rad=0` and checks if the new result gives a valid segment, updating `hit_seg` and `hit_pnt` accordingly.

But in the OCaml version, there's a critical difference: it ignores the `_new_hit_pnt` value and only updates `hit_seg`, never updating `hit_pnt` when the retry succeeds. This is a mismatch between the two implementations. which could cause position differences. But I need to check if this scenario actually happens in practice—where the initial call fails to find a segment but the retry with zero radius succeeds. Looking at the conditions, this is an edge case where the hit point with radius falls outside the segment, but removing the radius gives a more centered point that lands inside one. This is definitely a real bug in the OCaml implementation.

So I need to update the hit_pnt when the retry succeeds.

content.text:
I found a bug! In the OCaml FVI `find_vector_intersection_v2`, when the retry with `rad=0` succeeds, C updates both `hit_seg` AND `hit_pnt`, but OCaml only updates `hit_seg` and explicitly ignores the new hit point.

Let me verify:

C line 908: `hit_pnt = new_hit_pnt;` — updates the hit point.

Now OCaml:

Line 1008: `ignore _new_hit_pnt;` — explicitly discards the retry's hit point.

This is the bug. When the retry with `rad=0` succeeds, C uses the new hit point, but OCaml keeps using the old one. Let me fix this.

let hit_type, hit_pnt, hit_seg2, seglist, n_segs =
      fvi_sub_v2
        ~collision_table ~n_objects ~is_d2 ~game_time ~ignore_obj_list
        ~game_mode_coop ~player_objnum ~physics_cheat
        st ~p0 ~startseg ~p1 ~rad ~thisobjnum ~flags ~entry_seg:(-2)
    in
    let hit_pnt_x, hit_pnt_y, hit_pnt_z = hit_pnt in
    (* Resolve hit_seg *)
    let hit_seg = ref (-1) in
    if hit_seg2 <> -1
    then (
      let sd2 = Effect.perform (Ox_gameseg.Fetch_segment_data hit_seg2) in
      let sv2, st2, n2, svp2 = unpack_seg_for_masks sd2 in
      let _, _, cm2 =
        Ox_gameseg.get_seg_masks
          ~checkp:hit_pnt ~rad:0 ~seg_verts:sv2 ~side_types:st2 ~normals:n2 ~seg_vert_positions:svp2
      in
      if cm2 = 0 then hit_seg := hit_seg2);
    if !hit_seg = -1
    then
      hit_seg := find_point_seg_fvi_v2 ~point:hit_pnt ~segnum:startseg ~n_segments;
    (* HACK: if HIT_WALL and hit_seg=-1, try fvi_hit_seg2 *)
    if hit_type = hit_wall && !hit_seg = -1 && st.fvi_hit_seg2 <> -1
    then (
      let sd2 = Effect.perform (Ox_gameseg.Fetch_segment_data st.fvi_hit_seg2) in
      let sv2, st2, n2, svp2 = unpack_seg_for_masks sd2 in
      let _, _, cm2 =
        Ox_gameseg.get_seg_masks
          ~checkp:hit_pnt ~rad:0 ~seg_verts:sv2 ~side_types:st2 ~normals:n2 ~seg_vert_positions:svp2
      in
      if cm2 = 0 then hit_seg := st.fvi_hit_seg2);
    (* Retry with rad=0 if hit_seg still -1 *)
    if !hit_seg = -1
    then (
      let st2 = make_fvi_state () in
      st2.segs_visited.(0) <- startseg;
      st2.n_segs_visited <- 1;
      st2.fvi_hit_seg2 <- -1;
      let _new_hit_type, _new_hit_pnt, new_hit_seg2, _new_seglist, _new_n_segs =
        fvi_sub_v2
          ~collision_table ~n_objects ~is_d2 ~game_time ~ignore_obj_list
          ~game_mode_coop ~player_objnum ~physics_cheat
          st2 ~p0 ~startseg ~p1 ~rad:0 ~thisobjnum ~flags ~entry_seg:(-2)
      in
      ignore _new_hit_pnt;
      if new_hit_seg2 <> -1 then hit_seg := new_hit_seg2);
    (* Trim seglist at hit point *)
    let final_seglist = Array.create ~len:max_fvi_segs 0 in
    let final_n_segs = ref n_segs in
    Array.blit ~src:seglist ~src_pos:0 ~dst:final_seglist ~dst_pos:0 ~len:n_segs;
